# Oggi — Product Backlog

**Date:** 2026-05-06

| EP-001 | Sprint & Backlog Management | 5 | 12 | MVP |
| EP-002 | Developer Daily View | 3 | 8 | MVP |

**Total story points:** 65
**MVP stories:** 12 (35pt)

---

## Prioritization Notes

- The MVP critical path is **EP-001 → EP-002 → EP-003 → EP-004**: each epic unlocks the next demonstrable increment (Valentina can plan → devs can act → devs can check-in → Valentina sees status).
- All MVP stories are **HIGH** except **US-011 (Silent auto-flagging)**, which is MEDIUM because its visible value depends on the team dashboard being already in place.
- The **priority rationale** (US-005) is treated as HIGH despite not blocking technically: it is the persona-winning feature for Sara per the PRD, and skipping it would erode the product differentiator on day one.
- Growth stories (EP-005, EP-006) are **LOW**, deliberately deferred until the MVP produces real adoption signal — velocity, blocker detection, and Kanban integration all rely on accumulated usage data.
- Vision stories (EP-007) are **LOW** and high-level only — they require historical sprint and check-in data that the MVP must first generate.

---

## Epics & User Stories

---

### EP-001: Sprint & Backlog Management

> Valentina creates the sprint, manages developer profiles, and curates the prioritized task backlog with rationale notes — the source of truth that drives everything else in Oggi.
> **Scope:** MVP | **Stories:** 5 | **Story Points:** 12

---

#### US-001: Create a sprint

**Epic:** EP-001 | **Priority:** HIGH | **Story Points:** 2

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want to create a sprint with a name, start date, and end date,
so that I can define the time window in which the team will plan and execute work.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: open the sprint management page, fill out a form, and see the newly created sprint persist as the active sprint.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Submitting a form with name, start date, and end date creates and persists a new sprint
- [ ] Sprint name is required; start date must precede end date — invalid input shows a validation error and does not create the sprint
- [ ] The newly created sprint is shown as the active sprint in the management UI
- [ ] Only authenticated users (Valentina) can access the sprint creation page

---

#### US-002: Manage developer profiles with shareable access link

**Epic:** EP-001 | **Priority:** HIGH | **Story Points:** 3

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want to create, edit, and delete developer profiles (name, role, seniority) and receive a unique shareable URL for each,
so that I can onboard each team member with a single link they can open without signing up.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: create a dev profile, copy its unique URL, and share it — the link is ready to be used by the dev surface (built in EP-002).

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] User can create a profile with name, role (frontend/backend), and seniority (junior/senior)
- [ ] On creation, a unique URL containing a UUID v4 token is generated, displayed, and copyable
- [ ] The token (and resulting URL) remains stable across edits to name/role/seniority
- [ ] User can edit profile attributes
- [ ] Deleting a profile is a soft-delete: the profile is marked inactive, its access URL stops working immediately, it disappears from task-assignment dropdowns, but its historical commitments, check-ins, and retrospectives remain intact and visible in past sprints and reports
- [ ] User can regenerate the access token in one click; the old URL stops working immediately while the profile and its history remain intact
- [ ] All active profiles are listed in a management view, each showing its shareable URL; soft-deleted profiles are accessible from a separate "archived" view

---

#### US-003: Add tasks to the sprint backlog

**Epic:** EP-001 | **Priority:** HIGH | **Story Points:** 3

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want to add tasks (title, description, assigned dev) to the active sprint,
so that I can build up the work the team will pick up day by day.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: open the sprint, add multiple tasks each assigned to a dev, and see them appear in the backlog list.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] User can add a task with title (required), description (optional), and an assigned dev profile picked from existing profiles
- [ ] Tasks appear in the sprint's backlog list immediately after creation
- [ ] User can edit a task's title, description, and assignee, and the changes persist
- [ ] User can delete a task; deletion is reflected in the backlog list
- [ ] An unassigned task cannot be saved (assignee is required)

---

#### US-004: Reorder tasks by priority

**Epic:** EP-001 | **Priority:** HIGH | **Story Points:** 3

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want to reorder backlog tasks via drag-and-drop or by setting an explicit position,
so that the most important tasks appear first when each developer opens their daily view.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: drag a task to a new position in the backlog, reload the page, and see the new order preserved — and that order is what dev pages will read from.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Dragging a task in the backlog list and dropping it at a new position persists the new order
- [ ] User can also set a numeric priority position via an inline input
- [ ] Order is preserved across page reloads and sessions
- [ ] Reordering is restricted to tasks within the same sprint

---

#### US-005: Add a priority rationale note to a task

**Epic:** EP-001 | **Priority:** HIGH | **Story Points:** 1

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want to attach a free-text rationale to each task explaining why it is prioritized,
so that developers — especially senior ones — understand the reasoning, not just the order.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: open a task, write a rationale note, save it, and see it persist on the task — ready to be displayed on the dev page (US-007).

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Each task has an editable plain-text rationale field accessible from the task detail or list
- [ ] The rationale is capped at 500 characters; the editor shows a live character counter
- [ ] Saving the rationale persists the value; clearing it leaves an empty (allowed) rationale
- [ ] The rationale is visible alongside the task in the management view

---

### EP-002: Developer Daily View

> Each developer opens their personal page via a unique link with no authentication, sees today's tasks ordered by priority with rationale notes, and declares a morning commitment that turns into an explicit daily contract.
> **Scope:** MVP | **Stories:** 3 | **Story Points:** 8

---

#### US-006: Open dev daily page via unique URL

**Epic:** EP-002 | **Priority:** HIGH | **Story Points:** 2

**Story**
As Filippo,
I want to open my personal page using the link Valentina sent me,
so that I can see my work without creating an account or signing in.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: paste the shared URL into a browser and see a page that recognizes them by name — the entry point for everything the dev does in EP-002 and EP-003.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Navigating to the unique URL with a valid token shows a page identifying the dev by name
- [ ] An invalid or unknown token shows a clear "link not valid" message and no protected content
- [ ] No login or signup step is ever requested on the dev surface
- [ ] The token is opaque (UUID v4) and cannot be guessed by enumeration

---

#### US-007: View today's tasks ordered by priority with rationale

**Epic:** EP-002 | **Priority:** HIGH | **Story Points:** 3

**Story**
As a developer,
I want to see my tasks for today ordered by priority with the rationale notes from Valentina,
so that I know what to work on and why it matters before I commit.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: open their page and see a list of their tasks for the active sprint, sorted by priority, each with the rationale visible.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] The page lists only the tasks assigned to this dev for the active sprint
- [ ] Tasks are displayed in priority order as set in the management UI
- [ ] Each task shows the rationale note (if present) directly under the task title
- [ ] When no tasks are assigned, an empty-state message is shown instead of an empty list

---

#### US-008: Declare morning commitment

**Epic:** EP-002 | **Priority:** HIGH | **Story Points:** 3

**Story**
As a developer,
I want to multi-select the tasks I will work on today and confirm my commitment in a single action,
so that I declare an explicit daily contract instead of drifting through the backlog.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: tick a subset of today's tasks, press a single Confirm button, and see the page change to display "today's plan" — the foundation for the check-in loop in EP-003.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] The dev can multi-select any subset (at least one) of today's tasks and confirm in a single action
- [ ] Once confirmed, the commitment is persisted and shown as "today's plan" on the page
- [ ] The dev can change the commitment until the mid-day check-in is triggered
- [ ] Submitting an empty commitment is not allowed and produces an inline message
- [ ] Each commitment is scoped to a single calendar day per dev (one per day)

---

### EP-003: Check-in & Retrospective

> The lightweight commitment-and-check-in loop that surfaces blockers early and closes each day with an explicit retrospective — the behavioral core of Oggi.
> **Scope:** MVP | **Stories:** 3 | **Story Points:** 10

---

#### US-009: Respond to mid-day check-in

**Epic:** EP-003 | **Priority:** HIGH | **Story Points:** 5

**Story**
As a developer,
I want to respond at mid-day for each task I committed to with a status of On Track / Blocked / Task Changed,
so that I can surface my real state without having to attend a meeting.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: at the configured mid-day time, open their page, see a check-in prompt for each committed task, submit statuses, and have those statuses persist for Valentina to see.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] At the scheduled mid-day time, the dev page shows a check-in prompt for each committed task
- [ ] For each task the dev can pick exactly one status: On Track, Blocked, or Task Changed
- [ ] Selecting Blocked allows an optional free-text note
- [ ] Submitted check-ins are persisted and timestamped
- [ ] Once submitted, the prompt is replaced by a summary of the responses on the same page

---

#### US-010: Submit end-of-day retrospective

**Epic:** EP-003 | **Priority:** HIGH | **Story Points:** 3

**Story**
As a developer,
I want to mark each committed task as Done / In Progress / Not Started at end-of-day,
so that I close my day with an honest, lightweight self-report.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: at the configured end-of-day time, open their page, mark each committed task with one of three outcomes, and close the day.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] At the scheduled end-of-day time, the dev page surfaces a retrospective form with each committed task
- [ ] For each task the dev picks exactly one outcome: Done, In Progress, or Not Started
- [ ] Once submitted, the day is closed and outcomes are persisted; further changes are not accepted
- [ ] The retrospective is reachable only after the end-of-day trigger time

---

#### US-011: Auto-flag silent developers

**Epic:** EP-003 | **Priority:** MEDIUM | **Story Points:** 2

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want the system to automatically mark a developer as Silent when they have not responded to a check-in within 2 hours,
so that I can spot disengagement without manual chasing.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: observe that a dev who skipped check-in is automatically flagged Silent in the data, ready to be displayed in the team dashboard (US-012).

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] If a dev has not responded to a mid-day check-in within 2 hours of its scheduled time, their status is set to Silent
- [ ] The Silent status is computed automatically without manual action
- [ ] When the dev finally submits the check-in, the Silent status is replaced by the submitted status

---

### EP-004: Team Dashboard

> Valentina's real-time window into team state — current task per dev, last check-in status, and open blockers — turning Oggi into a continuous standup substitute.
> **Scope:** MVP | **Stories:** 1 | **Story Points:** 5

---

#### US-012: View real-time team status grid

**Epic:** EP-004 | **Priority:** HIGH | **Story Points:** 5

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want a real-time grid showing every developer's current task, last check-in status, and any open blockers,
so that I have full visibility of where the team stands at any moment without micromanaging.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: open the team dashboard and watch it update live as devs commit, check in, change tasks, or get auto-flagged Silent — all without refreshing.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] The dashboard shows one row per active dev profile with: name, today's committed task(s), last check-in status, and any blocker note — soft-deleted profiles are excluded
- [ ] The dashboard scope is "today" only in MVP; navigation to past days is out of scope and deferred to the sprint summary report (US-017)
- [ ] The grid updates in real time when a dev commits, checks in, or submits the retro — no refresh required
- [ ] Blocked and Silent devs are visually distinguished from On Track ones (e.g., color or badge)
- [ ] Only authenticated users (Valentina) can access the dashboard

---

### EP-005: Authentication & Integrations

> Post-MVP capabilities that move Oggi from a single-PO tool to a multi-account product integrated with the team's existing toolchain.
> **Scope:** Growth | **Stories:** 2 | **Story Points:** 8

---

#### US-013: Sign in as a developer with OAuth

**Epic:** EP-005 | **Priority:** LOW | **Story Points:** 3

**Story**
As a developer,
I want to sign in to Oggi with my GitHub or Google account,
so that I have a personal authenticated account instead of relying on a shared link.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: sign in via OAuth, be linked to their existing dev profile, and access their daily page as an authenticated user (the token-based URL continues to work in parallel).

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Devs can sign in with GitHub or Google OAuth on the dev surface
- [ ] On first sign-in, the user is matched to an existing dev profile (by email or admin-driven association)
- [ ] Authenticated devs can access the same daily page features as token-based ones
- [ ] Token-based access remains operational alongside authenticated access for backward compatibility

---

#### US-014: Import tasks from Jira / Linear

**Epic:** EP-005 | **Priority:** LOW | **Story Points:** 5

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want to import tasks into the Oggi backlog from Jira or Linear via a webhook integration,
so that I do not have to duplicate tasks across tools.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: connect a Jira or Linear project, see tasks appear in the Oggi backlog, and assign them to devs without manual re-entry.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] User can connect a Jira or Linear workspace via a configuration page
- [ ] Selected tasks from the external system are mirrored into the Oggi backlog with title, description, and source link
- [ ] Updates to the source task (title, status) are reflected in Oggi within a reasonable delay
- [ ] Disconnecting the integration stops further imports without deleting already imported tasks

---

### EP-006: Analytics & Insights

> Reporting capabilities that turn the daily commitment + check-in stream into actionable signals about team velocity, blockers, and sprint outcomes.
> **Scope:** Growth | **Stories:** 3 | **Story Points:** 9

---

#### US-015: Track per-developer velocity

**Epic:** EP-006 | **Priority:** LOW | **Story Points:** 3

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want to see how many tasks each dev completes per sprint over time,
so that I can plan future sprints with realistic capacity per person.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: open a velocity view, select a dev, and see their completed tasks per sprint as a trend.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] A velocity view aggregates completed (Done) tasks per dev per sprint
- [ ] Trends are visualized over at least the last 5 sprints
- [ ] Velocity per dev is exportable as CSV

---

#### US-016: Detect blockers automatically

**Epic:** EP-006 | **Priority:** LOW | **Story Points:** 3

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want the system to flag tasks that are likely blocked even without an explicit Blocked status,
so that I can intervene before a dev falls silent.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: see in the dashboard automatic blocker flags on tasks that have been In Progress for multiple consecutive days without a Done outcome, alongside any explicit blockers.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Tasks that remain In Progress across multiple end-of-day retros without progress are flagged as likely-blocked
- [ ] Likely-blocked flags are visually distinguishable from explicit Blocked statuses
- [ ] Detection rules are configurable (e.g., threshold of consecutive days)

---

#### US-017: Generate sprint summary report

**Epic:** EP-006 | **Priority:** LOW | **Story Points:** 3

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want a sprint summary report at sprint end with completed vs. committed tasks, top blockers, and silent days per dev,
so that I can share an objective recap with stakeholders.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: open a closed sprint and view a single-page report aggregating all the meaningful sprint data, exportable as PDF or shareable as a link.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] At sprint end, the report aggregates: total committed, total Done, top blockers, silent days per dev
- [ ] The report is exportable (PDF or link)
- [ ] Past sprint reports are accessible from a sprint history view

---

### EP-007: AI-powered Planning

> A future tier where accumulated sprint data feeds AI-driven planning aids — reducing the planning load on Valentina and helping devs match the right task to the right energy level.
> **Scope:** Vision | **Stories:** 3 | **Story Points:** 13

---

#### US-018: AI-suggested task priorities

**Epic:** EP-007 | **Priority:** LOW | **Story Points:** 5

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want AI-generated priority suggestions for new tasks based on past sprint data and dependencies,
so that I spend less time prioritizing and more time validating the suggestions.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: add a task and see an AI-suggested priority position with a one-line rationale, which Valentina can accept or override.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] When a new task is added, an AI suggestion proposes its priority position with a short rationale
- [ ] Valentina can accept the suggestion in one click or override it
- [ ] Suggestions improve measurably as more sprint data accumulates

---

#### US-019: Automatic effort estimation

**Epic:** EP-007 | **Priority:** LOW | **Story Points:** 5

**Story**
As Valentina,
I want the system to suggest an effort estimate for each new task based on similar past tasks,
so that I can plan sprint capacity with less manual estimation.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: add a task and see an estimated effort (in hours or points) with a confidence level, derived from past similar tasks.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] When a task is added, the system suggests an effort estimate alongside a confidence indicator
- [ ] The estimate cites the historical tasks it was derived from
- [ ] Valentina can accept or override the estimate

---

#### US-020: Mood-based task recommendations

**Epic:** EP-007 | **Priority:** LOW | **Story Points:** 3

**Story**
As a developer,
I want to optionally report my energy or mood at morning commitment time and receive task recommendations matched to it,
so that I tackle the right kind of work at the right moment of the day.

**Demonstrates**
After implementing this story, the user can: at morning commitment, optionally pick an energy level, and see tasks reordered or highlighted to match (e.g., deep-focus tasks for high energy).

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] At morning commitment, the dev can optionally pick an energy level (low / medium / high)
- [ ] Today's tasks are highlighted or reordered to match the reported energy
- [ ] The dev can ignore the recommendation and commit normally
- [ ] No mood data is shown to Valentina or other devs (privacy by default)

---

## Backlog Assumptions & Open Questions

> _This section lists assumptions made during backlog generation and questions left open for the team._

- **[ASSUMPTION]** Mid-day and end-of-day trigger times are configured per sprint (or globally), but the exact times are not specified in the PRD — implementation should make them configurable.
- **[ASSUMPTION]** Only one sprint is "active" at any moment; activation derives from the sprint's start/end dates without a manual activation step.
- **[ASSUMPTION]** Tasks belong exclusively to one sprint — there is no cross-sprint reuse.
- **[ASSUMPTION]** Developer profiles persist across sprints; each sprint can have a different subset of devs assigned tasks.
- **[DECISION]** A single organization-wide timezone is configured at install time. It governs both the cron triggers (mid-day, end-of-day, silent auto-flag) and the meaning of "today" on the dev daily view — single source of truth. Per-dev timezones are deferred to Growth.
- **[ASSUMPTION]** A developer's commitment is locked-in only at the moment of the mid-day check-in; before that, devs can edit their commitment freely.
- **[DECISION]** Sprints become read-only 24 hours after their `endDate`. During the 24h grace period Valentina can still correct commitments, check-ins, and retrospectives; after that, sprint data is immutable to keep velocity (US-015) and summary reports (US-017) reliable.
- **[DECISION]** Tokens are revoked via a "Regenerate token" action on the dev profile (one click). The old URL stops working immediately; the profile and its historical data are preserved. This contains the leakage risk noted in ADR-1 without forcing token expiration on every dev.
- **[DECISION]** The priority rationale is plain text, capped at 500 characters. Rich formatting and markdown are explicitly out of scope: the value is the content, not the styling. Auto-linkifying URLs may be revisited in Growth if needed.
- **[DECISION]** Deleting a dev profile is a soft-delete: the profile is marked inactive (URL stops working, removed from task-assignment dropdowns), but its historical commitments, check-ins, and retrospectives are preserved so velocity (US-015) and sprint summaries (US-017) remain accurate. Hard-delete is not exposed in MVP.
- **[DECISION]** The MVP team dashboard shows only today's state. Historical views (rolling N-day, drill-down) are deferred to EP-006 (sprint summary, velocity) — keeping the dashboard a focused real-time signal, not a reporting surface.

---
